metadata.title: HashiCorp Terraform Associate TA-003 Practice Questions

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: It allows infrastructure to be defined, versioned, and managed using code instead of manual processes
It eliminates the need for cloud providers
It replaces the need for networking knowledge
It automatically scales applications without configuration
questions.explanation: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) allows you to define infrastructure in configuration files that can be versioned, shared, and reused. This replaces manual, error-prone processes with repeatable, automated provisioning.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of IaC over manual infrastructure provisioning?
options: IaC requires no testing before deployment
IaC configurations can be stored in version control for auditability and collaboration
IaC eliminates all infrastructure costs
IaC prevents all security vulnerabilities automatically
questions.explanation: Storing IaC configurations in version control systems like Git enables change tracking, code review, collaboration, and rollback capabilities -- key advantages over manual, undocumented provisioning.

questions.question: What does idempotency mean in the context of Infrastructure as Code?
options: Applying the same configuration multiple times produces the same result without unintended side effects
Infrastructure can only be created once and never modified
Each configuration run creates duplicate resources
Configuration files must be written in a specific programming language
questions.explanation: Idempotency means that applying the same IaC configuration multiple times produces the same end state. Terraform achieves this by comparing desired state (configuration) with actual state (state file) and only making necessary changes.

questions.question: An organization discovers that their manually configured production servers have drifted from their documented architecture. Which IaC benefit directly addresses this problem?
options: Cost optimization
Drift detection and remediation
Load balancing
Automatic scaling
questions.explanation: Infrastructure drift occurs when actual infrastructure diverges from its intended state. IaC tools like Terraform detect drift by comparing the desired configuration against the real-world state and can remediate by applying changes to bring infrastructure back into compliance.

questions.question: What is the difference between declarative and imperative approaches to IaC?
options: Declarative defines the desired end state; imperative defines the step-by-step commands to reach that state
Declarative is faster; imperative is more secure
Declarative only works with AWS; imperative works with all providers
There is no meaningful difference between the two approaches
questions.explanation: Declarative IaC (like Terraform and CloudFormation) describes the desired end state and lets the tool figure out how to achieve it. Imperative IaC (like Ansible playbooks or shell scripts) specifies the exact sequence of commands to execute. Terraform uses a declarative approach.

questions.question: Which of the following describes immutable infrastructure?
options: Infrastructure that cannot be monitored after deployment
Infrastructure components are replaced rather than modified in place when changes are needed
Infrastructure that uses only physical servers, never virtual machines
Infrastructure that requires manual approval for every change
questions.explanation: Immutable infrastructure means that once a server or component is deployed, it is never modified. Instead, a new version is built from scratch and the old one is destroyed. This eliminates configuration drift and ensures consistency. Terraform supports this pattern through resource replacement.

questions.question: A team uses Terraform to manage infrastructure across development, staging, and production environments. They want identical infrastructure in all three environments with only minor parameter differences. Which IaC pattern best achieves this?
options: Maintaining completely separate Terraform configurations for each environment
Using the same Terraform modules with different variable values per environment via workspaces or separate variable files
Manually cloning resources from development to production in the cloud console
Writing a shell script that runs Terraform with hardcoded values for each environment
questions.explanation: Reusable modules with environment-specific variable files (e.g., dev.tfvars, prod.tfvars) or workspaces allow teams to maintain a single source of truth while customizing per-environment parameters like instance sizes, replica counts, or naming conventions.

questions.question: Which IaC practice most effectively enables disaster recovery by allowing infrastructure to be recreated in a different region quickly?
options: Keeping manual runbooks with step-by-step instructions
Taking regular snapshots of all virtual machines
Storing all infrastructure definitions in version-controlled IaC that can be applied to any target region
Maintaining a hot standby manually configured to match production
questions.explanation: Version-controlled IaC enables rapid disaster recovery because the entire infrastructure can be recreated in any region by running the same configurations with updated region parameters. This is faster and more reliable than manual rebuilding or relying on VM snapshots that may not capture networking and IAM configurations.

questions.question: How does IaC improve security compliance for infrastructure?
options: IaC automatically patches all operating systems
IaC enables policy-as-code scanning and peer review of infrastructure changes before they are applied
IaC encrypts all data at rest by default
IaC eliminates the need for access controls
questions.explanation: IaC enables security compliance by allowing infrastructure changes to be reviewed via pull requests, scanned with policy-as-code tools (like Sentinel or OPA), and audited through version control history. This shift-left approach catches misconfigurations before they reach production.

questions.question: A DevOps engineer argues that their shell scripts accomplish the same goals as Terraform. Which limitation of imperative scripts does Terraform's declarative approach specifically solve?
options: Shell scripts cannot interact with cloud provider APIs
Shell scripts require the author to handle ordering, error recovery, and partial-failure states manually, while Terraform builds a dependency graph and manages this automatically
Shell scripts are always slower than Terraform
Shell scripts cannot be stored in version control
questions.explanation: Imperative scripts require explicit ordering of operations, manual error handling, and rollback logic for partial failures. Terraform's declarative model builds a dependency graph, determines the correct order of operations automatically, and handles partial failures gracefully by tracking state.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical benefit of Infrastructure as Code?
options: Reproducibility across environments
Version-controlled change history
Elimination of all human error
Automated provisioning and teardown
questions.explanation: While IaC significantly reduces human error through automation and code review, it does not eliminate all human error. Misconfigured Terraform code can still cause outages. IaC's primary benefits are reproducibility, version control, automation, and collaboration.

questions.question: An organization wants to enforce that all S3 buckets have encryption enabled before infrastructure is provisioned. At which stage in the IaC lifecycle should this validation occur?
options: After the infrastructure is deployed, using a monitoring tool
During the plan phase, using policy-as-code tools like Sentinel or OPA to validate the planned changes
Only during annual security audits
When the developer writes the code, relying solely on their knowledge
questions.explanation: Policy-as-code tools evaluate Terraform plans before apply, acting as automated gates in the CI/CD pipeline. This prevents non-compliant infrastructure from ever being provisioned, which is more effective than post-deployment detection that requires remediation.

questions.question: What is Terraform?
options: A configuration management tool like Ansible that configures software on existing servers
An infrastructure provisioning tool that uses a declarative language to define and manage cloud and on-premises resources
A container orchestration platform similar to Kubernetes
A monitoring and alerting tool for cloud infrastructure
questions.explanation: Terraform is an infrastructure provisioning tool by HashiCorp that uses the declarative HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) to define, plan, and create infrastructure across multiple cloud providers and services.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key advantage of Terraform being provider-agnostic?
options: It can only manage AWS resources
It uses a single workflow and language to manage resources across AWS, Azure, GCP, and hundreds of other providers
It replaces the need for cloud provider accounts
It automatically selects the cheapest cloud provider
questions.explanation: Terraform's provider-agnostic architecture means you use the same HCL syntax and workflow (init, plan, apply) regardless of whether you are managing AWS EC2 instances, Azure VMs, GCP buckets, or Kubernetes resources. This simplifies multi-cloud and hybrid strategies.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Terraform's execution plan (terraform plan)?
options: To automatically apply all changes without review
To show what changes Terraform will make to infrastructure before actually applying them
To generate documentation for the infrastructure
To estimate the monthly cost of the infrastructure
questions.explanation: terraform plan creates an execution plan showing exactly what resources will be created, modified, or destroyed. This dry-run capability allows teams to review and approve changes before they affect real infrastructure, reducing the risk of unintended modifications.

questions.question: How does Terraform's resource graph improve infrastructure provisioning?
options: It creates visual diagrams for documentation
It determines the correct order to create, update, or destroy resources based on dependencies, and parallelizes independent operations
It limits provisioning to one resource at a time for safety
It prevents any resource from being deleted
questions.explanation: Terraform builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of all resources and their dependencies. This graph determines which resources must be created before others and which can be created simultaneously, enabling safe parallelization and correct ordering.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Terraform state?
options: To store provider credentials securely
To map real-world resources to your configuration and track metadata for performance optimization
To serve as a backup of your cloud infrastructure
To generate billing reports for each resource
questions.explanation: Terraform state is the mechanism that maps resources defined in your configuration to real-world infrastructure objects. It tracks resource IDs, attributes, and dependencies so Terraform knows what exists, what needs to change, and what to destroy.

questions.question: A company is choosing between Terraform and AWS CloudFormation. Which factor most favors choosing Terraform?
options: The company exclusively uses AWS with no plans to adopt other providers
The company uses a multi-cloud strategy with resources across AWS, Azure, and GCP that should be managed with a single tool and workflow
The company wants the deepest possible integration with AWS-specific services on day one
The company prefers YAML over HCL for configuration
questions.explanation: Terraform's key differentiator over CloudFormation is its multi-cloud support. If an organization manages resources across multiple cloud providers, Terraform provides a unified workflow and language. CloudFormation is AWS-only, so it may be preferable for pure-AWS shops that want the tightest native integration.

questions.question: What is the difference between Terraform Community Edition (open-source) and Terraform Cloud?
options: There is no difference; they are the same product
Community Edition is a CLI tool that runs locally, while Terraform Cloud adds remote state management, team collaboration, policy enforcement, and a web UI
Community Edition only works with AWS; Terraform Cloud works with all providers
Terraform Cloud is the free version; Community Edition requires a paid license
questions.explanation: Terraform Community Edition (CE) is the free, open-source CLI. Terraform Cloud adds managed remote state, VCS integration, team access controls, Sentinel policy enforcement, run pipelines, and a web interface for collaboration. Terraform Enterprise is the self-hosted version of Terraform Cloud.

questions.question: Which Terraform edition is required to use Sentinel policies for infrastructure governance?
options: Terraform Community Edition (open-source)
Terraform Cloud (Team & Governance tier) or Terraform Enterprise
Any edition -- Sentinel is available in all versions
Sentinel is a third-party tool unrelated to Terraform
questions.explanation: Sentinel policy-as-code is a HashiCorp Enterprise feature available in Terraform Cloud's Team & Governance tier and Terraform Enterprise. It enables organizations to define mandatory policies (e.g., require tags, restrict instance types) that must pass before applies are allowed.

questions.question: How does Terraform differ from configuration management tools like Ansible or Chef?
options: Terraform provisions infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) while configuration management tools configure software on existing servers
Terraform and Ansible are identical tools that serve the same purpose
Configuration management tools can provision infrastructure but Terraform cannot
Terraform requires agents on managed servers while Ansible does not
questions.explanation: Terraform excels at provisioning infrastructure resources (VMs, networks, databases, load balancers). Configuration management tools like Ansible, Chef, and Puppet excel at configuring software on existing servers (installing packages, managing files, starting services). They are often used together.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Terraform dependency lock file (.terraform.lock.hcl)?
options: To prevent multiple users from running terraform apply simultaneously
To lock specific provider versions so that all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the same provider binaries
To encrypt the Terraform state file
To lock the Terraform CLI to a specific version
questions.explanation: The dependency lock file (.terraform.lock.hcl) records the exact provider versions and their cryptographic hashes. This ensures reproducible builds across different machines and CI/CD runs. It should be committed to version control so all team members use identical provider versions.

questions.question: Which language does Terraform use for its configuration files?
options: YAML
JSON only
HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)
Python
questions.explanation: Terraform uses HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL), a declarative language designed specifically for defining infrastructure. HCL is human-readable and supports features like variables, expressions, and functions. Terraform also accepts JSON as an alternative syntax.

questions.question: What is HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) primarily used for?
options: Writing Terraform configurations in a GUI editor
Providing remote state management, team collaboration, VCS-driven workflows, and policy enforcement for Terraform runs
Replacing cloud provider APIs with a universal API
Monitoring application performance metrics
questions.explanation: HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) provides a managed platform for Terraform operations including remote state storage, workspace management, VCS integration for plan-on-PR workflows, team access controls, Sentinel policy enforcement, and a private module registry.

questions.question: What is a Terraform provider?
options: A cloud vendor that offers free Terraform licenses
A plugin that allows Terraform to interact with a specific infrastructure platform or service API
A team member who writes Terraform code
A backup service for Terraform state files
questions.explanation: A Terraform provider is a plugin that implements resource types and data sources for a specific infrastructure platform (e.g., aws, azurerm, google, kubernetes). Providers translate HCL configurations into API calls to the target platform.

questions.question: Where are Terraform providers downloaded from by default?
options: GitHub releases
The Terraform Registry (registry.terraform.io)
Docker Hub
The HashiCorp website downloads page
questions.explanation: By default, terraform init downloads providers from the public Terraform Registry at registry.terraform.io. Organizations can also configure private registries or filesystem mirrors for air-gapped environments.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a resource block in Terraform?
options: To define the Terraform version to use
To declare a piece of infrastructure that Terraform should create and manage
To import existing infrastructure into Terraform
To specify output values for display
questions.explanation: A resource block declares an infrastructure object (e.g., aws_instance, azurerm_virtual_network) that Terraform should create and manage. It specifies the resource type, a local name, and configuration arguments that define its properties.

questions.question: What is the difference between a resource and a data source in Terraform?
options: Resources are for AWS only; data sources work with all providers
Resources create and manage infrastructure; data sources read information from existing infrastructure or external sources without managing it
Data sources are deprecated in favor of resources
There is no difference; they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Resources (resource blocks) tell Terraform to create and manage infrastructure. Data sources (data blocks) let Terraform read information from infrastructure that exists outside of Terraform's management, like looking up an AMI ID, reading an existing VPC, or fetching values from an external API.

questions.question: What does the required_providers block inside a terraform block specify?
options: The minimum number of providers that must be configured
The source location and version constraints for each provider the configuration depends on
Which cloud accounts Terraform has permission to access
The order in which providers should be initialized
questions.explanation: The required_providers block within the terraform block specifies each provider's source address (e.g., hashicorp/aws) and version constraints (e.g., ~> 5.0). This ensures terraform init downloads the correct provider versions from the correct registry.

questions.question: Which block is used to configure provider-specific settings like region and credentials in Terraform?
options: resource block
variable block
provider block
terraform block
questions.explanation: The provider block configures a specific provider with settings like region, credentials, default tags, or endpoint URLs. For example, the AWS provider block typically includes the region argument. Multiple provider blocks can configure the same provider with different aliases for multi-region deployments.

questions.question: A Terraform configuration references provider 'aws' with version constraint '~> 5.0'. Which of the following versions would this constraint allow?
questions.explanation: The ~> (pessimistic constraint) operator allows the rightmost version component to increment. ~> 5.0 allows any version >= 5.0.0 and < 6.0.0. So 5.32.1 is valid, but 4.99.0 (too old) and 6.0.0 (next major version) are not.

questions.question: What file extension do Terraform configuration files use?
options: .yaml
.tf
.hcl
.tfstate
questions.explanation: Terraform configuration files use the .tf extension (e.g., main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf). While Terraform also supports .tf.json for JSON syntax, .tf with HCL is the standard convention. The .tfstate extension is used for state files, not configuration.

questions.question: What happens when you define multiple .tf files in the same directory?
options: Only the file named main.tf is processed; others are ignored
Terraform concatenates all .tf files in the directory into a single configuration
Each .tf file creates a separate Terraform workspace
Terraform throws an error if more than one .tf file exists
questions.explanation: Terraform processes all .tf files in the working directory as a single configuration. The separation into files (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf) is a convention for organization, not a technical requirement. Terraform merges them all before processing.

questions.question: What is a provider alias used for in Terraform?
options: Renaming a provider to a shorter name for convenience
Configuring multiple instances of the same provider with different settings, such as deploying resources to multiple AWS regions
Creating a backup provider in case the primary fails
Hiding the provider name from the state file for security
questions.explanation: Provider aliases allow you to configure the same provider multiple times with different settings. A common use case is deploying resources to multiple AWS regions by creating aliased provider configurations (e.g., provider 'aws' with alias 'us-west-2' and provider 'aws' with alias 'eu-west-1').

questions.question: Which Terraform feature allows you to look up the latest Amazon Machine Image (AMI) ID matching specific filters instead of hardcoding it?
options: A resource block with a dynamic lookup
A data source (data block) that queries AWS for the AMI
A variable with a default value
The terraform import command
questions.explanation: Data sources (data blocks) query external information. A data 'aws_ami' block with filters (e.g., owner, name pattern) dynamically looks up the latest matching AMI at plan time, eliminating hardcoded IDs that become stale.

questions.question: A team needs to deploy the same application to both AWS and Azure using Terraform. What is the recommended approach for managing providers in this configuration?
options: Use a single universal provider that works with all clouds
Configure separate provider blocks for aws and azurerm in the same configuration, with resources specifying which provider to use
Create two completely separate Terraform projects with no shared code
Use Terraform only for AWS and a different tool for Azure
questions.explanation: Terraform supports multiple providers in a single configuration. You configure separate provider blocks (e.g., provider 'aws' and provider 'azurerm') and each resource implicitly uses its matching provider, or you can explicitly set the provider argument on a resource block.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the terraform block in a Terraform configuration?
options: To define infrastructure resources
To configure Terraform settings including required providers, required Terraform version, and backend configuration
To declare input variables
To specify output values
questions.explanation: The terraform block configures Terraform itself -- not infrastructure. It contains required_providers (provider sources and versions), required_version (Terraform CLI version constraints), backend configuration (where state is stored), and cloud block (for HCP Terraform integration).

questions.question: What does terraform init do?
options: Creates infrastructure resources in the cloud
Initializes the working directory by downloading providers, configuring the backend, and installing modules
Destroys all managed infrastructure
Formats the Terraform configuration files
questions.explanation: terraform init is the first command you run in a new or cloned Terraform configuration. It downloads and installs provider plugins, initializes the backend (state storage), downloads referenced modules, and creates the .terraform directory and lock file.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the .terraform directory?
options: It contains the Terraform state file
It is a cache directory created by terraform init that stores downloaded providers and modules -- it should not be committed to version control
It stores all Terraform configuration files
It is where Terraform writes its execution logs
questions.explanation: The .terraform directory is created by terraform init and contains cached provider plugins, downloaded modules, and backend configuration. It should be added to .gitignore because it contains large binaries and machine-specific paths. The lock file (.terraform.lock.hcl) should be committed, but not the .terraform directory itself.

questions.question: A Terraform configuration uses required_version = '>= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0'. A team member has Terraform 1.4.6 installed. What happens when they run terraform init?
options: Terraform automatically upgrades itself to version 1.5.0
Terraform init succeeds but displays a warning
Terraform init fails with an error stating the installed version does not meet the constraint
The required_version constraint is ignored during init
questions.explanation: The required_version constraint is checked during terraform init. If the installed CLI version (1.4.6) does not satisfy the constraint (>= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0), Terraform immediately fails with an error. The team member must install a compatible version before proceeding.

questions.question: What is a provisioner in Terraform, and what is HashiCorp's recommendation regarding their use?
options: Provisioners are the primary way to create resources; HashiCorp recommends using them for everything
Provisioners run scripts on resources after creation; HashiCorp recommends using them as a last resort because they break the declarative model
Provisioners automatically detect infrastructure drift
Provisioners are used to define provider configurations
questions.explanation: Provisioners (like local-exec and remote-exec) run scripts during resource creation or destruction. HashiCorp recommends using them only as a last resort because they introduce imperative steps, cannot be fully modeled in the plan, and can cause errors that leave resources in a partially-configured state.

questions.question: What is the difference between the local-exec and remote-exec provisioners?
options: local-exec runs commands on the machine running Terraform; remote-exec runs commands on the newly created resource via SSH or WinRM
local-exec is for Linux; remote-exec is for Windows
local-exec runs before resource creation; remote-exec runs after
They are identical in functionality
questions.explanation: local-exec runs a command on the machine where Terraform is executing (e.g., your workstation or CI/CD runner). remote-exec connects to the created resource via SSH or WinRM and runs commands on it. Both run during resource creation by default unless when = destroy is specified.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid Terraform resource meta-argument?
options: depends_on
lifecycle
provider
backend
questions.explanation: The valid resource meta-arguments are: depends_on (explicit dependencies), count (create multiple instances), for_each (create instances from a map/set), provider (select a non-default provider), lifecycle (customize behavior), and provisioner. Backend is configured in the terraform block, not on resources.

questions.question: What does the depends_on meta-argument do in a resource block?
options: It specifies that a resource depends on a specific Terraform version
It creates an explicit dependency between resources when Terraform cannot automatically detect the relationship
It prevents a resource from being destroyed
It sets the order in which providers are initialized
questions.explanation: depends_on declares explicit dependencies between resources when Terraform cannot infer the relationship from the configuration (e.g., when a resource depends on a side effect of another resource rather than on its attributes). Terraform normally builds its dependency graph from attribute references automatically.

questions.question: What is a Terraform variable used for?
options: Storing the Terraform state remotely
Parameterizing Terraform configurations so values can be customized without modifying the code
Defining cloud provider API endpoints
Scheduling Terraform runs at specific times
questions.explanation: Input variables (variable blocks) parameterize Terraform configurations. They allow values like instance types, region names, or CIDR blocks to be passed in at runtime via CLI flags, .tfvars files, environment variables, or defaults, making configurations reusable across environments.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct order of variable definition precedence in Terraform (highest to lowest)?
options: Default value > .tfvars file > environment variable > CLI -var flag
CLI -var flag > .auto.tfvars > terraform.tfvars > environment variable (TF_VAR_) > default value
Environment variable > CLI flag > .tfvars file > default value
Default value > CLI flag > environment variable > .tfvars file
questions.explanation: Terraform variable precedence from highest to lowest: CLI -var or -var-file flags > *.auto.tfvars (alphabetical) > terraform.tfvars > environment variables (TF_VAR_name) > default value in variable block. Higher precedence sources override lower ones.

questions.question: What does the terraform plan command do?
options: Creates all the resources defined in the configuration
Shows a preview of the changes Terraform would make without actually applying them
Deletes the Terraform state file
Formats the configuration files
questions.explanation: terraform plan compares the desired state (configuration files) with the current state (state file and real infrastructure) and outputs a detailed preview of what changes (create, update, destroy) Terraform would make. No actual changes are made to infrastructure.

questions.question: What does the terraform apply command do?
options: Only creates the execution plan
Executes the changes required to reach the desired state defined in the configuration
Validates the syntax of configuration files without making changes
Downloads provider plugins
questions.explanation: terraform apply creates an execution plan (like terraform plan) and then, upon approval, executes the planned changes to create, update, or destroy infrastructure resources to match the desired state in the configuration.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the -out flag in terraform plan?
options: To specify the output format (JSON or text)
To save the execution plan to a binary file that can be passed to terraform apply for exact execution
To write the plan output to a log file
To specify the output directory for generated resources
questions.explanation: terraform plan -out=tfplan saves the plan to a binary file. This saved plan can be passed to terraform apply tfplan to execute exactly the changes shown in the plan, with no approval prompt. This is critical in CI/CD to ensure the applied changes match what was reviewed.

questions.question: What does the -target flag do when used with terraform plan or terraform apply?
options: Specifies which cloud provider to target
Limits the operation to a specific resource or module, processing only it and its dependencies
Sets the target environment (dev, staging, production)
Specifies the target Terraform version
questions.explanation: The -target flag restricts Terraform operations to a specific resource (e.g., -target=aws_instance.web) or module. Only the targeted resource and its dependencies are included in the plan/apply. HashiCorp recommends using -target only for debugging or exceptional situations, not for routine operations.

questions.question: What does the terraform destroy command do?
options: Removes the Terraform configuration files from disk
Destroys all resources managed by the current Terraform configuration and state
Deletes only the state file but leaves resources intact
Removes Terraform from the system
questions.explanation: terraform destroy creates a plan to destroy all resources managed by the current state file and, upon approval, executes the destruction. It is the inverse of terraform apply. You can target specific resources with -target to avoid destroying everything.

questions.question: What does terraform validate do?
options: Checks that the configuration is syntactically valid and internally consistent without accessing any remote APIs or state
Validates that all resources exist in the cloud
Runs security scanning on the configuration
Validates that the state file matches the actual infrastructure
questions.explanation: terraform validate checks configuration files for syntax errors, invalid references, and type mismatches without accessing remote APIs, state, or provider functionality. It is faster than terraform plan and useful as an early CI/CD gate to catch obvious errors.

questions.question: What does terraform fmt do?
options: Converts Terraform files from HCL to JSON format
Rewrites Terraform configuration files to a canonical format and style
Formats the output of terraform plan for readability
Removes unused variables from configuration files
questions.explanation: terraform fmt rewrites .tf files to the canonical HCL format, standardizing indentation, alignment, and style. It is commonly run as a pre-commit hook or CI check to ensure consistent formatting across a team.

questions.question: What is the purpose of terraform import?
options: To import Terraform modules from the registry
To bring an existing infrastructure resource under Terraform management by adding it to the state file
To import variable values from an external file
To convert CloudFormation templates to Terraform
questions.explanation: terraform import associates an existing real-world resource with a resource block in your configuration by writing its current state into the Terraform state file. After import, Terraform manages the resource. Note: you must write the corresponding resource block manually before importing (or use import blocks in Terraform 1.5+).

questions.question: A team wants to replace a specific resource without modifying the configuration. Which approach should they use in Terraform 1.5+?
options: terraform taint (deprecated)
terraform apply -replace=RESOURCE_ADDRESS
terraform destroy -target=RESOURCE_ADDRESS followed by terraform apply
Manually delete the resource in the cloud console
questions.explanation: terraform apply -replace=RESOURCE_ADDRESS marks a resource for replacement (destroy and recreate) in the next apply. This replaced the deprecated terraform taint command. It is safer than manual deletion because it is done within a plan/apply cycle with state tracking.

questions.question: What does the -auto-approve flag do when used with terraform apply?
options: Automatically approves merge requests in version control
Skips the interactive approval prompt and applies changes immediately
Automatically rolls back changes if they fail
Approves the plan but delays execution until a scheduled time
questions.explanation: terraform apply -auto-approve skips the interactive yes/no confirmation prompt and immediately applies the planned changes. This is commonly used in CI/CD pipelines after a separate plan step has been reviewed. It should never be used interactively without reviewing the plan first.

questions.question: What does the terraform output command do?
options: Creates output files from the Terraform configuration
Displays the values of output variables defined in the configuration from the current state
Exports the Terraform plan to a file
Outputs the provider configuration
questions.explanation: terraform output reads output values from the state file and displays them. Output values are defined using output blocks and are useful for passing information between configurations (e.g., passing a VPC ID from a networking configuration to an application configuration).

questions.question: Which terraform workspace commands are used to manage workspaces?
options: terraform workspace init, terraform workspace deploy
terraform workspace new, terraform workspace select, terraform workspace list, terraform workspace delete
terraform workspace create, terraform workspace switch
terraform workspace add, terraform workspace remove
questions.explanation: Terraform workspace management commands include: new (create a workspace), select (switch to a workspace), list (show all workspaces), delete (remove a workspace), and show (display the current workspace). Workspaces allow multiple state files from a single configuration.

questions.question: What does the terraform show command display?
options: A list of all available providers
Human-readable output of the current state or a saved plan file
The Terraform CLI version information
A list of all available Terraform commands
questions.explanation: terraform show provides a human-readable representation of the current state file or a saved plan file. It is useful for inspecting what Terraform currently manages. Adding the -json flag produces machine-readable JSON output for programmatic consumption.

questions.question: Which command would you use to view all resources currently tracked in the Terraform state?
options: terraform plan
terraform state list
terraform show -resources
terraform list
questions.explanation: terraform state list outputs all resource addresses currently tracked in the state file. You can optionally filter with a pattern (e.g., terraform state list aws_instance). For detailed attributes of a specific resource, use terraform state show RESOURCE_ADDRESS.

questions.question: What is the purpose of terraform graph?
options: To display a chart of infrastructure costs over time
To generate a visual representation of the Terraform dependency graph in DOT format
To graph the performance metrics of managed resources
To show the git commit history of configuration files
questions.explanation: terraform graph outputs the dependency graph in DOT format, which can be rendered into a visual diagram using tools like Graphviz. This helps understand resource dependencies and the order in which Terraform creates or destroys resources.

questions.question: A team member accidentally runs terraform apply and creates unwanted resources. What is the quickest way to undo the change?
options: Run terraform apply again with the same configuration
Delete the state file and start over
Run terraform destroy -target for the unwanted resources, or revert the configuration and run terraform apply
Contact the cloud provider support team
questions.explanation: To undo unwanted changes, you can either target-destroy the specific resources (terraform destroy -target=RESOURCE_ADDRESS) or revert the configuration changes in version control and run terraform apply to reconcile the infrastructure with the previous desired state.

questions.question: Which command checks whether the Terraform configuration files are syntactically correct?
options: terraform check
terraform validate
terraform lint
terraform verify
questions.explanation: terraform validate checks configuration files for syntax errors, missing required arguments, and other structural issues. It does not access remote state or APIs, making it fast and suitable as an early CI/CD validation step.

questions.question: In a CI/CD pipeline, what is the recommended sequence for Terraform commands?
options: apply > plan > init
init > validate > plan -out=tfplan > (review/approve) > apply tfplan
plan > init > apply
validate > apply -auto-approve
questions.explanation: The recommended CI/CD sequence is: init (set up the working directory), validate (catch syntax errors early), plan -out=tfplan (generate and save the plan for review), review/approve (human or automated gate), then apply tfplan (apply the exact reviewed plan).

questions.question: What does the -refresh=false flag do when passed to terraform plan?
options: It prevents Terraform from writing any changes to the state file
It skips querying the real infrastructure for current state, using only the cached state file for the plan
It disables automatic provider updates
It prevents Terraform from refreshing the CLI version
questions.explanation: By default, terraform plan queries real infrastructure to update the state before planning. The -refresh=false flag skips this step, using only the existing state file. This is faster but may produce an inaccurate plan if infrastructure has changed outside of Terraform.

questions.question: What is the purpose of terraform plan -refresh-only?
options: It refreshes the Terraform CLI to the latest version
It detects drift by comparing real infrastructure to the state file and proposes state updates, without planning any configuration changes
It only refreshes provider plugins
It resets the state file to an empty state
questions.explanation: terraform plan -refresh-only compares real-world infrastructure against the state file and proposes state file updates to reflect any drift. It does not plan changes to match the configuration -- it only updates the state to match reality. This replaced the deprecated terraform refresh command.

questions.question: What does the terraform providers command display?
options: A list of all cloud providers available worldwide
The providers required by the current configuration and their configured version constraints
The pricing of each provider's services
The current health status of provider APIs
questions.explanation: terraform providers shows a tree of providers required by the current configuration, including their source addresses and version constraints. This helps verify that the correct providers are being used.

questions.question: What is a Terraform module?
options: A type of Terraform provider
A container for multiple Terraform resources that are used together, enabling reuse and organization
A monitoring dashboard for Terraform resources
A tool for testing Terraform configurations
questions.explanation: A Terraform module is a set of Terraform configuration files in a single directory. Modules allow you to group related resources together, parameterize them with variables, and reuse them across different configurations. Every Terraform working directory is a module (the root module).

questions.question: What is the root module in Terraform?
options: The first module published to the Terraform Registry
The module with the highest priority in the configuration
The set of Terraform configuration files in the directory where you run terraform commands
A special module provided by HashiCorp that all configurations must include
questions.explanation: The root module is the working directory where you run Terraform commands. It contains your main .tf files. Any modules called from the root module are child modules. Every Terraform configuration has exactly one root module.

questions.question: How do you call a child module from a root module in Terraform?
options: Using an import block
Using a module block with a source argument pointing to the module's location
Using a resource block with type set to 'module'
Using the terraform module install command
questions.explanation: You call a module using a module block with a source argument (e.g., source = './modules/vpc' for local or source = 'hashicorp/consul/aws' for registry). You also pass input variables as arguments within the module block.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid module source in Terraform?
options: A local file path, Terraform Registry, GitHub URL, S3 bucket, or GCS bucket
Only the Terraform Registry
Only local file paths
Only GitHub repositories
questions.explanation: Terraform modules can be sourced from: local paths (./modules/vpc), the Terraform Registry (hashicorp/consul/aws), GitHub/BitBucket URLs, generic Git repos, HTTP URLs, S3 buckets, and GCS buckets. The source argument determines where terraform init fetches the module from.

questions.question: How do you pass values into a child module?
options: By setting environment variables
By defining input variables in the module and setting their values in the module block of the calling configuration
By editing the module's source code directly
By using terraform import
questions.explanation: Child modules accept input through variable blocks defined within the module. The calling configuration sets these variables as arguments in the module block: module 'vpc' { source = './modules/vpc', cidr_block = '10.0.0.0/16' }.

questions.question: How do you access a value produced by a child module in the calling (root) module?
options: By reading the child module's state file directly
By referencing module.MODULE_NAME.OUTPUT_NAME, where the child module defines the value in an output block
By using a data source to query the child module
Child module values are automatically available without any configuration
questions.explanation: Child modules expose values through output blocks. The calling module accesses them with the syntax module.<MODULE_NAME>.<OUTPUT_NAME>. For example, module.vpc.vpc_id returns the vpc_id output from the vpc module. Only values defined as outputs are accessible outside the module.

questions.question: What happens to a module's variables and resources if they are not explicitly exposed through outputs?
options: They are automatically available to the calling module
They are encapsulated within the module and not accessible to the calling module
They are added to the global scope
Terraform throws an error during plan
questions.explanation: Modules in Terraform have their own variable scope. Internal resources and local values are encapsulated and not accessible outside the module unless explicitly exposed via output blocks. This encapsulation is a key design principle of modules.

questions.question: A team wants to use a module from the Terraform Registry but pin it to a specific version. Which argument in the module block achieves this?
options: required_version
version
module_version
pin_version
questions.explanation: The version argument in a module block pins a registry module to a specific version or version constraint: module 'vpc' { source = 'terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws', version = '~> 5.0' }. The version argument only works for modules from registries, not local paths or Git sources.

questions.question: What is the standard module structure recommended by HashiCorp?
options: A single file called module.tf containing all resources
main.tf (resources), variables.tf (input variables), outputs.tf (output values), and optionally a README.md
A directory for each resource type
An index.tf file that imports all other files
questions.explanation: The standard module structure includes: main.tf (primary resources), variables.tf (input variable declarations), outputs.tf (output value declarations), and README.md (documentation). Larger modules may also include versions.tf (provider requirements) and separate .tf files for logical groupings.

questions.question: What is the Terraform Public Module Registry naming convention for published modules?
options: module-name
terraform-PROVIDER-NAME (e.g., terraform-aws-vpc)
provider/module/version
There is no naming convention
questions.explanation: The Terraform Registry requires published modules to follow the naming convention terraform-<PROVIDER>-<NAME> (e.g., terraform-aws-vpc, terraform-google-network). This convention makes it clear which provider the module works with.

questions.question: What is a private module registry, and when would you use one?
options: A registry that only stores deprecated modules
A registry hosted within an organization (via Terraform Cloud/Enterprise or self-hosted) that shares modules internally without making them public
A module registry that requires payment to access
A registry that only works with private cloud providers
questions.explanation: Private module registries allow organizations to share Terraform modules internally. HCP Terraform and Terraform Enterprise include a built-in private registry. Teams publish vetted, approved modules that conform to organizational standards, enabling governance at scale.

questions.question: After adding a new module source to your configuration, what must you run before terraform plan?
options: terraform validate
terraform init (or terraform init -upgrade to update existing modules)
terraform fmt
terraform import
questions.explanation: When you add or change a module source, you must run terraform init to download the module. If updating an existing module to a new version, use terraform init -upgrade to fetch the newer version. Without init, Terraform does not have the module files and will error.

questions.question: A module creates an AWS VPC and returns the VPC ID as an output. Another module needs this VPC ID to create subnets. How should this dependency be structured?
options: Hardcode the VPC ID in the subnet module
Pass the VPC module's output as an input variable to the subnet module: module 'subnets' { vpc_id = module.vpc.vpc_id }
Use a data source in the subnet module to look up the VPC by name
Create both the VPC and subnets in the same module to avoid inter-module references
questions.explanation: The recommended pattern is to pass outputs from one module as inputs to another: the VPC module outputs vpc_id, and the subnet module accepts vpc_id as an input variable. This creates an implicit dependency and keeps modules decoupled and reusable.

questions.question: Can a module call other modules (nested modules)?
options: No, modules can only be called from the root module
Yes, modules can call other modules, creating a hierarchy of root and child modules
Only if the nested module is from the Terraform Registry
Only with Terraform Enterprise
questions.explanation: Modules can call other modules, creating a nested hierarchy. For example, a 'network' module might call a 'vpc' module and a 'subnets' module internally. However, deeply nested module structures can become complex to maintain, so keep nesting shallow when possible.

questions.question: What does terraform get do?
options: Downloads and installs the Terraform CLI binary
Downloads the modules referenced in the configuration into the .terraform directory
Retrieves the current state from the remote backend
Fetches the latest provider versions
questions.explanation: terraform get downloads the modules referenced in the configuration. However, terraform init also downloads modules (in addition to providers and backend configuration), so terraform get is rarely needed separately. Use terraform init -upgrade to update modules.

questions.question: A team has a module that creates 3 identical environments. They want to create multiple instances of this module with different parameters. Which meta-argument should they use?
options: count on the module block
for_each on the module block with a map of environment configurations
depends_on on the module block
Modules cannot be instantiated multiple times
questions.explanation: for_each on a module block creates multiple instances of the module, each with different parameters from a map. For example: module 'env' { for_each = { dev = {...}, staging = {...}, prod = {...} } ... }. This is preferred over count for named instances because it uses map keys as identifiers, which is more stable.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using module composition over monolithic configurations?
options: Monolithic configurations are always better because they have fewer files
Module composition enables reuse, testing in isolation, reduced blast radius, and clearer separation of concerns
Module composition is only for large enterprises
There is no benefit -- modules add unnecessary complexity
questions.explanation: Module composition breaks large configurations into smaller, reusable, testable units. Benefits include: code reuse across projects, independent testing and development, reduced blast radius (changes to one module do not affect others), and clearer ownership and separation of concerns.

questions.question: What is the difference between a module's input variables and the root module's input variables?
options: There is no difference in how they are defined
Root module variables can be set via CLI, environment variables, or .tfvars files; child module variables can only be set by the calling module block
Child module variables have higher precedence than root variables
Root module variables cannot have default values
questions.explanation: Root module variables can be set from multiple external sources: CLI -var flags, .tfvars files, environment variables (TF_VAR_), or defaults. Child module variables can only be set by the calling module block -- they cannot be set externally. This enforces encapsulation.

questions.question: Where can you find pre-built, community-maintained Terraform modules?
options: Docker Hub
The Terraform Public Module Registry at registry.terraform.io
npm (Node Package Manager)
The AWS Marketplace
questions.explanation: The Terraform Public Module Registry (registry.terraform.io) hosts thousands of community and partner-maintained modules for common infrastructure patterns (VPCs, EKS clusters, Azure resource groups). Modules have documentation, version history, and input/output specifications.

questions.question: What are the benefits of using modules from the Terraform Registry instead of writing your own?
options: Registry modules are always more secure than custom modules
Registry modules provide tested, documented patterns that reduce development time and follow best practices, but should be reviewed before use in production
Registry modules are the only way to use Terraform
Registry modules automatically update themselves to the latest version
questions.explanation: Registry modules accelerate development by providing pre-built patterns, but they should be evaluated for quality, security, and fit before production use. Always pin versions, review the module's source code and issues, and verify it meets your organization's standards.

questions.question: A module author wants to prevent users from passing invalid CIDR blocks. How can they validate the cidr_block variable?
options: Add a comment explaining the expected format
Use a validation block inside the variable block with a condition that checks the format using can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0))
Set the variable type to 'cidr'
Variables cannot be validated in Terraform
questions.explanation: Terraform variable blocks support validation blocks with a condition (must evaluate to true) and an error_message. For CIDR validation: variable 'cidr_block' { validation { condition = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0)), error_message = 'Must be a valid CIDR block.' } }. This catches errors at plan time.

questions.question: What are the three stages of the core Terraform workflow?
options: Build, Test, Deploy
Write, Plan, Apply
Init, Validate, Destroy
Code, Review, Merge
questions.explanation: The core Terraform workflow is Write (author configuration), Plan (preview changes), and Apply (execute changes). This cycle is repeated for every infrastructure change and is central to how Terraform operates.

questions.question: In the Terraform workflow, what happens during the 'Write' phase?
options: Terraform automatically generates configuration files
You author Terraform configuration files that define the desired infrastructure state
Terraform writes changes to the cloud provider
Terraform writes the execution plan to a file
questions.explanation: During the Write phase, you create or modify .tf files that declare the infrastructure you want. This is a human activity -- writing HCL code that describes resources, variables, providers, and their configurations.

questions.question: How does the Terraform workflow change when using Terraform Cloud compared to local CLI?
options: The workflow is completely different with no similarities
The Write-Plan-Apply cycle remains the same, but plans and applies run remotely, state is stored centrally, and team collaboration features are added
Terraform Cloud only supports the Apply phase
Terraform Cloud eliminates the need for configuration files
questions.explanation: Terraform Cloud keeps the same Write-Plan-Apply workflow but adds: remote execution (plans and applies run on Terraform Cloud, not your laptop), remote state storage, VCS integration (plan on PR, apply on merge), team access controls, and policy checks between plan and apply.

questions.question: In a VCS-driven workflow with Terraform Cloud, when does a terraform plan run automatically?
options: Every hour on a schedule
When a pull request is opened or updated against the connected branch
Only when manually triggered through the Terraform Cloud UI
When a new team member is added to the workspace
questions.explanation: In a VCS-driven workflow, Terraform Cloud automatically runs terraform plan when a pull request is opened or updated, allowing team members to review the planned changes as part of the code review process. Apply typically runs automatically when the PR is merged to the target branch.

questions.question: Why should Terraform configurations be stored in version control (e.g., Git)?
options: Terraform requires Git to function
Version control provides change history, collaboration, code review, rollback capability, and audit trails for infrastructure changes
Version control makes Terraform runs faster
It is only recommended for Terraform Enterprise users
questions.explanation: Storing Terraform configurations in version control is a best practice because it provides: full change history (who changed what and when), collaboration through branches and pull requests, code review before changes are applied, rollback to previous known-good configurations, and an audit trail for compliance.

questions.question: What is the recommended Git branching strategy for Terraform changes?
options: Commit all changes directly to the main branch
Create feature branches, open pull requests, review the terraform plan output, then merge to main to trigger apply
Use a separate repository for each environment
Never use branches -- keep everything in a single commit
questions.explanation: The recommended strategy is: create a feature branch for each change, open a PR, review the terraform plan output (either locally or via Terraform Cloud's PR integration), get team approval, then merge to main. The merge can trigger terraform apply automatically in CI/CD.

questions.question: A team uses Terraform Cloud with a VCS-driven workflow. A speculative plan on a PR shows unexpected resource destruction. What should the team do?
options: Merge the PR quickly before anyone notices
Review the plan carefully, investigate why the destruction is planned, fix the configuration in the PR, and re-run the plan before merging
Disable Terraform Cloud and switch to local execution
Approve the plan and deal with consequences later
questions.explanation: Speculative plans on PRs exist precisely to catch unexpected changes before they happen. The team should investigate the root cause (drift, configuration error, provider upgrade side effect), fix the issue in the branch, and verify with a new plan before merging.

questions.question: What is a Terraform Cloud workspace?
options: A physical server that runs Terraform
A managed environment that stores state, variables, and run history for a specific Terraform configuration
A UI theme for the Terraform Cloud dashboard
A temporary sandbox that is deleted after each run
questions.explanation: A Terraform Cloud workspace is a persistent environment that contains: a specific Terraform configuration (linked to a VCS repo), its own state file, workspace-specific variables, run history, and access controls. Workspaces map to environments or components.

questions.question: In Terraform Cloud, what is the difference between a speculative plan and a normal plan?
options: There is no difference
A speculative plan runs on PRs and cannot be applied; a normal plan runs on the main branch and can be applied after approval
A speculative plan is faster because it skips validation
A speculative plan only runs on Terraform Enterprise
questions.explanation: Speculative plans are triggered by pull requests and are for preview only -- they cannot be applied. Normal plans run when changes are merged to the connected branch and can be confirmed and applied. This ensures that only reviewed, merged changes reach production.

questions.question: What is cost estimation in Terraform Cloud?
options: A tool that estimates the time required to provision resources
An automatic calculation of estimated monthly cloud costs for the planned infrastructure changes
A feature that optimizes resource configurations for lowest cost
A billing dashboard for Terraform Cloud subscription costs
questions.explanation: Terraform Cloud's cost estimation feature automatically calculates the estimated monthly cost impact of planned infrastructure changes. This helps teams understand the financial impact of their changes before applying them, supporting budget governance.

questions.question: Which file should NOT be committed to version control in a Terraform project?
options: main.tf
terraform.tfstate (state file)
variables.tf
.terraform.lock.hcl
questions.explanation: The terraform.tfstate file should never be committed to version control because it may contain sensitive data (passwords, keys, IP addresses). State should be stored in a remote backend (S3, Terraform Cloud, etc.). The .terraform.lock.hcl file SHOULD be committed to ensure consistent provider versions.

questions.question: A team wants Sentinel policies to block any apply that creates an AWS S3 bucket without encryption enabled. At which point in the Terraform Cloud workflow does Sentinel run?
options: Before terraform init
After terraform plan but before terraform apply (between plan and apply)
After terraform apply completes
During terraform validate
questions.explanation: Sentinel policies in Terraform Cloud run after the plan phase but before the apply phase. They evaluate the planned changes and can enforce (hard-mandatory), warn (soft-mandatory), or advise (advisory). Hard-mandatory failures block the apply entirely.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using run triggers in Terraform Cloud?
options: They automatically delete old state files
They allow one workspace's successful apply to automatically trigger a run in a dependent workspace
They trigger alerts when resources are manually modified
They schedule runs at specific times
questions.explanation: Run triggers create dependencies between workspaces. When a source workspace completes a successful apply, it automatically starts a new run in connected downstream workspaces. This is useful for chaining infrastructure changes (e.g., networking workspace triggers compute workspace).

questions.question: In a multi-team organization, how should Terraform workspaces be structured to balance autonomy and governance?
options: One giant workspace for all teams and all infrastructure
Separate workspaces per team or component with shared modules from a private registry, Sentinel policies for governance, and RBAC for access control
Each developer gets their own workspace with no shared standards
Workspaces should never be used in multi-team environments
questions.explanation: Best practice is to create separate workspaces per team or component, use a private module registry for shared, vetted modules, apply Sentinel policies for organizational governance (naming, tagging, security), and use RBAC to control who can plan and apply in each workspace.

questions.question: What is the purpose of terraform login?
options: To log into a cloud provider account
To authenticate the Terraform CLI with Terraform Cloud or Terraform Enterprise by obtaining an API token
To create a new HashiCorp account
To enable two-factor authentication for Terraform
questions.explanation: terraform login authenticates the Terraform CLI with Terraform Cloud (or Enterprise) by opening a browser to generate an API token, which is stored locally in ~/.terraform.d/credentials.tfrc.json. This enables CLI-driven workflows with remote state, remote execution, and the private registry.

questions.question: What role does code review play in the Terraform workflow?
options: Code review is not applicable to infrastructure code
Code review ensures that Terraform changes are reviewed by peers for correctness, security, and best practices before being applied
Code review is only needed for application code
Code review automatically fixes Terraform errors
questions.explanation: Code review for Terraform changes serves the same purpose as application code review: peer validation of correctness, security posture, naming conventions, and best practices. Reviewing the terraform plan output alongside the code changes is especially valuable to catch unintended impacts.

questions.question: A Terraform apply fails midway through, successfully creating 3 out of 5 resources. What happens to the state file?
options: The state file is rolled back to its pre-apply state
The state file is updated to reflect only the 3 successfully created resources; subsequent apply attempts will try to create the remaining 2
The state file becomes corrupted and must be manually repaired
All 3 successfully created resources are automatically destroyed
questions.explanation: Terraform updates the state file incrementally as resources are created. If apply fails midway, the state file reflects what was actually created. Running terraform apply again will attempt to create only the remaining resources that failed. Terraform does not roll back successfully created resources automatically.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the .gitignore file in a Terraform project?
options: It tells Terraform which resources to ignore
It specifies files and directories that Git should not track, such as .terraform/, *.tfstate, *.tfvars (with secrets), and crash.log
It is required for Terraform to function
It lists providers that should not be used
questions.explanation: A Terraform project's .gitignore should exclude: .terraform/ (cached providers/modules), *.tfstate and *.tfstate.* (state files with sensitive data), *.tfvars (may contain secrets), crash.log, and override.tf files. The .terraform.lock.hcl should NOT be in .gitignore.

questions.question: What is a no-operation (no-op) plan in Terraform, and what does it indicate?
options: A plan that cannot be generated due to errors
A plan that shows no changes are needed because the current infrastructure matches the desired configuration
A plan that only destroys resources
A plan generated without provider credentials
questions.explanation: A no-op plan (showing 'No changes. Infrastructure is up-to-date.') means the real infrastructure matches the desired state in the configuration. This indicates the infrastructure is fully converged and no actions are needed. It is the expected outcome when running plan with no configuration changes.

questions.question: Why is it important to run terraform plan before terraform apply?
options: terraform plan is required or apply will not work
Running plan first lets you preview exactly what changes will be made, catch mistakes, and avoid unintended resource modifications or deletions
terraform plan makes apply run faster
terraform plan upgrades providers before apply
questions.explanation: Running terraform plan before apply is a safety practice that shows you exactly what Terraform intends to change. You can catch unintended deletions, unexpected changes, or misconfigured resources before they affect real infrastructure. While not technically required, it is strongly recommended.

questions.question: How can you automate Terraform runs in a CI/CD pipeline?
options: By manually running terraform commands on each developer's laptop
By using CI/CD tools (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc.) to run init, validate, plan on PRs, and apply -auto-approve on merge with proper state management and secrets handling
CI/CD pipelines cannot run Terraform
By scheduling Terraform runs once a month
questions.explanation: CI/CD automation for Terraform typically includes: running init/validate/plan on pull requests (for review), and init/apply with a saved plan on merge to main. Secrets are injected via CI/CD variables, and state is stored in a remote backend. -auto-approve is used in CI since approval happens in the PR review.

questions.question: What is Terraform state?
options: The current running status of your cloud services
A file that maps your Terraform configuration to real-world infrastructure resources, tracking their IDs, attributes, and dependencies
A backup of your Terraform configuration files
The version history of your Terraform code
questions.explanation: Terraform state (stored in terraform.tfstate) is a JSON file that maps each resource in your configuration to a real-world infrastructure object. It stores resource IDs, attributes, metadata, and dependencies so Terraform knows what exists and what needs to change.

questions.question: Why is the Terraform state file considered sensitive?
options: It contains the Terraform source code
It may contain sensitive data such as passwords, access keys, and IP addresses of managed resources in plaintext
It is too large to share via email
It uses a proprietary format that cannot be read
questions.explanation: The Terraform state file stores resource attributes including sensitive values like database passwords, access keys, and private IP addresses in plaintext. This is why state should never be committed to version control and should be stored in a secure remote backend with encryption and access controls.

questions.question: What is the difference between local state and remote state in Terraform?
options: Local state is more secure than remote state
Local state is stored in a terraform.tfstate file on disk; remote state is stored in a shared backend (S3, Terraform Cloud, etc.) enabling collaboration and locking
Remote state is only available with Terraform Enterprise
There is no functional difference
questions.explanation: Local state stores the state file on the user's filesystem. Remote state stores it in a shared backend (S3+DynamoDB, Azure Blob, GCS, Terraform Cloud) which enables: team collaboration (shared state), state locking (prevent concurrent modifications), encryption at rest, and access controls.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common remote backend for Terraform state?
options: GitHub repository
Amazon S3 with DynamoDB for state locking
Local filesystem with NFS mount
Email attachment
questions.explanation: Amazon S3 is one of the most common remote backends for Terraform state. When paired with a DynamoDB table for state locking, it provides durable, encrypted, versioned state storage with concurrent access protection. Other popular backends include Azure Blob Storage, GCS, and Terraform Cloud.

questions.question: What is state locking in Terraform?
options: Encrypting the state file so it cannot be read
Preventing concurrent operations on the same state to avoid corruption and conflicts
Locking resources so they cannot be modified in the cloud console
Pinning the state file to a specific Terraform version
questions.explanation: State locking prevents multiple users or processes from modifying the same state file simultaneously, which could cause data corruption or conflicting changes. Most remote backends (S3+DynamoDB, Terraform Cloud, Azure Blob) support automatic state locking.

questions.question: A terraform apply operation fails, and the state lock is not released. What command can be used to manually remove the lock?
options: terraform unlock
terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID
terraform state unlock
terraform init -unlock
questions.explanation: terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID manually removes a stuck state lock. The LOCK_ID is shown in the error message. This should only be used when you are certain no other operation is running, as force-unlocking during an active operation can cause state corruption.

questions.question: What does the terraform state mv command do?
options: Moves the state file to a different directory
Moves a resource from one address to another within the state, useful for refactoring configurations without destroying and recreating resources
Migrates state from local to remote backend
Moves resources between cloud providers
questions.explanation: terraform state mv renames a resource address in the state file. For example, if you rename a resource from aws_instance.old to aws_instance.new in your configuration, running terraform state mv aws_instance.old aws_instance.new updates the state to match without destroying the actual resource.

questions.question: What does the terraform state rm command do, and when would you use it?
options: It destroys the resource in the cloud and removes it from state
It removes a resource from the state file without destroying the actual infrastructure, effectively 'unmanaging' it
It removes the state file entirely
It removes the resource from the configuration files
questions.explanation: terraform state rm removes a resource from Terraform's state without destroying the actual infrastructure. The resource continues to exist in the cloud but Terraform no longer manages it. This is useful when you want to stop managing a resource with Terraform or move it to a different state file.

questions.question: How do you configure a remote backend in Terraform?
options: By setting an environment variable
By adding a backend block inside the terraform block in the configuration
By creating a backends.tf file with special syntax
Remote backends are configured automatically
questions.explanation: Remote backends are configured inside the terraform block: terraform { backend 's3' { bucket = 'my-terraform-state', key = 'prod/terraform.tfstate', region = 'us-east-1', dynamodb_table = 'terraform-locks' } }. After adding or changing a backend, run terraform init to migrate state.

questions.question: What happens when you change the backend configuration and run terraform init?
options: Terraform creates a new, empty state file in the new backend
Terraform offers to migrate the existing state to the new backend
Terraform refuses to change backends
The existing state is automatically deleted
questions.explanation: When terraform init detects a backend change, it prompts you to migrate the existing state to the new backend. If you confirm, it copies the state file to the new location. You can also use terraform init -migrate-state to explicitly trigger migration.

questions.question: What are Terraform workspaces (CLI workspaces) used for?
options: Creating separate UI dashboards for different teams
Managing multiple instances of state from a single configuration, commonly used for maintaining separate environments like dev, staging, and production
Running Terraform in different programming languages
Isolating provider credentials per environment
questions.explanation: CLI workspaces allow a single Terraform configuration to manage multiple, independent instances of state. Each workspace has its own state file. The current workspace name is available via terraform.workspace, enabling conditional logic per environment. Note: Terraform Cloud workspaces are a different, more feature-rich concept.

questions.question: Why do some practitioners recommend against using CLI workspaces for managing production environments?
options: CLI workspaces have a bug that corrupts production state
CLI workspaces share the same configuration and credentials, providing weak isolation -- separate root modules with different backends offer stronger environment boundaries
CLI workspaces cannot be used with remote backends
CLI workspaces do not support any cloud providers in production
questions.explanation: CLI workspaces share the same code, backend configuration, and credentials. A mistake in one workspace can affect another. Separate root modules per environment (with separate backends, variable files, and potentially different credentials) provide stronger isolation and clearer blast radius for production workloads.

questions.question: What does the terraform state pull command do?
options: Pulls the latest changes from a Git repository
Downloads and outputs the current state from the remote backend to stdout
Pulls resources from one cloud provider to another
Downloads the latest Terraform version
questions.explanation: terraform state pull retrieves the current state from the configured backend and outputs it to stdout as JSON. This is useful for inspecting remote state, creating backups, or piping state data to other tools for analysis.

questions.question: What does the terraform state push command do?
options: Pushes Terraform code to a Git repository
Uploads a local state file to the configured remote backend, overwriting the current remote state
Pushes resources to a cloud provider
Sends state notifications to team members
questions.explanation: terraform state push uploads a local state file to the remote backend. This is an advanced operation used for disaster recovery or manual state manipulation. It should be used with extreme caution as it overwrites the remote state.

questions.question: An organization needs to share the VPC ID from their networking Terraform configuration with their application Terraform configuration. What is the recommended approach?
options: Hardcode the VPC ID in the application configuration
Use the terraform_remote_state data source or outputs from a shared workspace to reference the networking state's output values
Copy the networking state file into the application directory
Use a shared global variable file
questions.explanation: The terraform_remote_state data source allows one configuration to read outputs from another configuration's state. The networking config defines a vpc_id output, and the application config reads it via data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.vpc_id. This creates a clean dependency between configurations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of state file versioning in remote backends like S3?
options: To track which Terraform version was used
To maintain a history of previous state files so you can recover from accidental corruption or deletion
To version the Terraform configuration files
To automatically upgrade the state file format
questions.explanation: Enabling versioning on the S3 bucket that stores Terraform state allows you to recover previous versions of the state file in case of corruption, accidental deletion, or bad applies. This is a critical safety net for state file management.

questions.question: What is the moved block in Terraform 1.1+ used for?
options: Moving resources between cloud providers
Declaring resource address changes within the configuration so Terraform automatically updates the state instead of destroying and recreating
Moving state files between backends
Migrating from Terraform to CloudFormation
questions.explanation: The moved block (e.g., moved { from = aws_instance.old, to = aws_instance.new }) tells Terraform that a resource was renamed in the configuration.
